name: Belsetán Aragonese (Bielsa valley)

ancestors.notes: Belsetán is a Central/Eastern High-Aragonese valley variety inheriting the Aragonese grapheme→IPA system; it declares the geminate-lateral/nasal deltas and documents lexical/morphological distinctiveness in notes.
Hispanic Vulgar Latin, inherited via Aragonese (an).
Gascon adstrate: Bielsa borders the Gascon-speaking Pyrenees; the -LL- → -r- pronoun development (er, ers) is attributed to Gascon contact.

notes: Belsetán — the Aragonese of the Bielsa valley (Valle de Bielsa, Sobrarbe, Huesca), a conservative Central/Eastern High-Aragonese variety (the classic monograph is Badía Margarit 1950, 'El habla del valle de Bielsa'). PHONOLOGICALLY-EXPRESSED DELTAS modelled here: PRESERVED GEMINATES. Bielsa is the one Ibero-Romance area that keeps Latin geminate -LL- and -NN- as long consonants in inherited (non-learned) words, written ⟨l·l⟩ and ⟨n·n⟩ in dialectal orthography: bel·la [ˈbelːa] 'alguna', payel·la [paˈjelːa], pen·na [ˈpenːa] 'peña', capan·na [kaˈpanːa] 'cabaña' (Badía Margarit 1950, via es.wikipedia 'Aragonés belsetano', secondary). Modelled by adding ⟨l·l⟩ → [lː] and ⟨n·n⟩ → [nː] graphemes (middot U+00B7); plain ⟨ll⟩ = [ʎ] and ⟨n⟩ = [n] are inherited unchanged. Belsetán keeps the Western/Central profile from an (F- preserved, /θ/ distinción, ⟨ch⟩=[tʃ]). FEATURES NOT PHONEME-MAPPABLE (documented, not modelled — Aragonese spelling is phonemic and these are lexical/etymological): Latin -CT- → -t- (FACTU > feto, not the general Aragonese feito) and -ULT- → -ut- (MULTU > muto), NOCTE > nuet — the words are simply spelled with ⟨t⟩ so the base map reads them; -LL- → -r- in the third-person pronouns er, ers (él, ellos); article el/la/es/las with allomorphs o/a/os/as; archaic pronouns nusaltros/vusaltros; atonic strong-perfect verb forms tenié, trayé, benié. Diphthongisation Ĕ/Ŏ → ie/ue inherited (Várvaro, AFA XLVI-XLVII, p. 250 places Bielsa inside the modern uá area). PROVENANCE HONESTY: the geminate feature is drawn from the Spanish-Wikipedia dialect article (secondary) which cites Badía Margarit (1950); the primary monograph was not directly obtainable. Várvaro (AFA) read directly.

sources.author: Badía Margarit, A. M.
sources.title: El habla del valle de Bielsa (Pirineo aragonés)
sources.notes: The classic Belsetán monograph; source of the preserved -LL-/-NN- geminates and the -CT-/-ULT- reductions. Cited via the secondary Spanish-Wikipedia dialect article; primary not directly obtainable in this work.

sources.author: Várvaro, A.
sources.title: De la escritura al habla: la diptongación de O breve tónica en el Alto Aragón
sources.notes: Read directly. Places Bielsa inside the modern uá diphthong area (p. 250).
